channel.title: Chrome Story[RSS+]
channel.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
channel.itunes:summary:
Chrome and Chromebook Guides and Tutorials

http://feeds.feedburner.com/ChromeStory
channel.description:
Chrome and Chromebook Guides and Tutorials

http://feeds.feedburner.com/ChromeStory

item.title: Google Adds ‘Compose’ Button to Chrome’s New Tab Page
item.itunes:title: Google Adds ‘Compose’ Button to Chrome’s New Tab Page
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Google is adding a new ‘Compose’ button to Chrome’s new tab page, bringing it right and center to your Chrome experience.

A new Chrome flag showed up in the Chromium code last week but didn’t do anything till today:

NTP Compose Box – Shows the Compose button on the New Tab Page Searchbox.

As of today, I can see the Compose button on the new tab page. However, I cannot do anything with it, yet.

So, why am I writing this article to share the news?

Mostly because of the placement of this new compose button – right next to big search field in the new tab page.

This is how it might work. You will type your prompt similar to your search query. Instead of search, you will ask Chrome to compose.

For example, you might type ‘write an email to apply for leave’ and then hit Compose. Chrome can then help you write the email for you.

That’s my best guess at the moment. Being where it is, and being built into Chrome, there are endless possibilities on how this could work. I’ll keep a close watch on this feature as Google enables the button. Check back this page for more updates, coming soon your way.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Google is adding a new ‘Compose’ button to Chrome’s new tab page, bringing it right and center to your Chrome experience.

A new Chrome flag showed up in the Chromium code last week but didn’t do anything till today:

NTP Compose Box – Shows the Compose button on the New Tab Page Searchbox.

As of today, I can see the Compose button on the new tab page. However, I cannot do anything with it, yet.

So, why am I writing this article to share the news?

Mostly because of the placement of this new compose button – right next to big search field in the new tab page.

This is how it might work. You will type your prompt similar to your search query. Instead of search, you will ask Chrome to compose.

For example, you might type ‘write an email to apply for leave’ and then hit Compose. Chrome can then help you write the email for you.

That’s my best guess at the moment. Being where it is, and being built into Chrome, there are endless possibilities on how this could work. I’ll keep a close watch on this feature as Google enables the button. Check back this page for more updates, coming soon your way.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Google is adding a new ‘Compose’ button to Chrome’s new tab page, bringing it right and center to your Chrome experience.

A new Chrome flag showed up in the Chromium code last week but didn’t do anything till today:

NTP Compose Box – Shows the Compose button on the New Tab Page Searchbox.

As of today, I can see the Compose button on the new tab page. However, I cannot do anything with it, yet.

So, why am I writing this article to share the news?

Mostly because of the placement of this new compose button – right next to big search field in the new tab page.

This is how it might work. You will type your prompt similar to your search query. Instead of search, you will ask Chrome to compose.

For example, you might type ‘write an email to apply for leave’ and then hit Compose. Chrome can then help you write the email for you.

That’s my best guess at the moment. Being where it is, and being built into Chrome, there are endless possibilities on how this could work. I’ll keep a close watch on this feature as Google enables the button. Check back this page for more updates, coming soon your way.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Delete a Bookmark in Chrome on iPhone
item.itunes:title: How to Delete a Bookmark in Chrome on iPhone
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Removing a bookmark in Chrome on your iPhone takes just a few steps. Whether you’re clearing out old links or reorganizing your saved pages, here’s how to do it.

Steps to Delete a Bookmark

  1. Open the Chrome app on your iPhone.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom right.
  3. Tap Bookmarks.
  4. Navigate to the folder where the bookmark is saved.
  5. Touch and slide the bookmark from the right edge toward the left.
  6. Tap Delete.

The bookmark will be removed immediately. There’s no undo option, so make sure you really want to delete it.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Removing a bookmark in Chrome on your iPhone takes just a few steps. Whether you’re clearing out old links or reorganizing your saved pages, here’s how to do it.

Steps to Delete a Bookmark

  1. Open the Chrome app on your iPhone.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom right.
  3. Tap Bookmarks.
  4. Navigate to the folder where the bookmark is saved.
  5. Touch and slide the bookmark from the right edge toward the left.
  6. Tap Delete.

The bookmark will be removed immediately. There’s no undo option, so make sure you really want to delete it.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Removing a bookmark in Chrome on your iPhone takes just a few steps. Whether you’re clearing out old links or reorganizing your saved pages, here’s how to do it.

Steps to Delete a Bookmark

  1. Open the Chrome app on your iPhone.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom right.
  3. Tap Bookmarks.
  4. Navigate to the folder where the bookmark is saved.
  5. Touch and slide the bookmark from the right edge toward the left.
  6. Tap Delete.

The bookmark will be removed immediately. There’s no undo option, so make sure you really want to delete it.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Use Read Aloud in Microsoft Edge
item.itunes:title: How to Use Read Aloud in Microsoft Edge
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Read Aloud in Microsoft Edge can read web pages out loud for you. It’s built in, easy to use, and works great whether you’re multitasking, want to give your eyes a break, or need help with accessibility. Here’s how to use it and make the most of its features.

Start Read Aloud on Any Web Page

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Navigate to a page with text—an article, blog post, or even a PDF.
  3. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  4. Select Read aloud from the menu.

Edge will begin reading the page out loud from the top. A control bar appears at the top of the page.

Use the Read Aloud Controls

The control bar includes:

  • Play/Pause: Start or stop the voice.
  • Skip Back/Forward: Move to the previous or next paragraph.
  • Voice Options: Change the voice and adjust the reading speed.

Click the gear icon to slow down or speed up the voice, or to choose from available voice types like Microsoft Aria or Guy.

Highlight and Start from a Specific Paragraph

If you don’t want to start at the top:

  1. Scroll to the paragraph you want to hear.
  2. Right-click anywhere in the text.
  3. Select Read aloud from here.

Edge will begin reading from that spot.

Use Read Aloud in PDF Files

  1. Open a PDF file in Edge.
  2. Click the Read aloud icon in the toolbar at the top.
  3. Edge will begin reading the text in the PDF, just like it does on web pages.

This is helpful for long documents, reports, or ebooks.

Change the Voice or Speed

  1. While Read Aloud is active, click the Voice options button (a gear icon).
  2. Use the dropdown to pick a different voice.
  3. Adjust the Speed slider to make it faster or slower.

Some voices sound more natural than others, so try a few to find the one you like best.

Keyboard Shortcuts for Read Aloud

  • Ctrl+Shift+U: Start or stop Read Aloud.
  • Use the on-screen buttons for skip, play, or pause.

There’s no shortcut for changing voices—you’ll need to do that manually through the options.

Supported Content

Read Aloud works on:

  • News sites and blogs
  • Wikipedia pages
  • PDFs
  • Online textbooks and articles

It may not work well on pages with mostly images, complex layouts, or dynamic content (like Google Docs).

Use Read Aloud on iPhone or Android

  1. Open the Edge app.
  2. Navigate to the page you want to hear.
  3. Tap the three-dot menu.
  4. Tap Read Aloud.

The app will begin reading, and you’ll see simple playback controls at the bottom of the screen.

This works on most article-style pages and PDFs opened inside Edge.

Final Tip

Combine Read Aloud with Immersive Reader for a clutter-free reading and listening experience. Just click the book icon in the address bar (if available) to enter Immersive Reader, then start Read Aloud from there.

Did you find this article helpful? Let me know by dropping a comment below. If you have questions, ask them away.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Read Aloud in Microsoft Edge can read web pages out loud for you. It’s built in, easy to use, and works great whether you’re multitasking, want to give your eyes a break, or need help with accessibility. Here’s how to use it and make the most of its features.

Start Read Aloud on Any Web Page

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Navigate to a page with text—an article, blog post, or even a PDF.
  3. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  4. Select Read aloud from the menu.

Edge will begin reading the page out loud from the top. A control bar appears at the top of the page.

Use the Read Aloud Controls

The control bar includes:

  • Play/Pause: Start or stop the voice.
  • Skip Back/Forward: Move to the previous or next paragraph.
  • Voice Options: Change the voice and adjust the reading speed.

Click the gear icon to slow down or speed up the voice, or to choose from available voice types like Microsoft Aria or Guy.

Highlight and Start from a Specific Paragraph

If you don’t want to start at the top:

  1. Scroll to the paragraph you want to hear.
  2. Right-click anywhere in the text.
  3. Select Read aloud from here.

Edge will begin reading from that spot.

Use Read Aloud in PDF Files

  1. Open a PDF file in Edge.
  2. Click the Read aloud icon in the toolbar at the top.
  3. Edge will begin reading the text in the PDF, just like it does on web pages.

This is helpful for long documents, reports, or ebooks.

Change the Voice or Speed

  1. While Read Aloud is active, click the Voice options button (a gear icon).
  2. Use the dropdown to pick a different voice.
  3. Adjust the Speed slider to make it faster or slower.

Some voices sound more natural than others, so try a few to find the one you like best.

Keyboard Shortcuts for Read Aloud

  • Ctrl+Shift+U: Start or stop Read Aloud.
  • Use the on-screen buttons for skip, play, or pause.

There’s no shortcut for changing voices—you’ll need to do that manually through the options.

Supported Content

Read Aloud works on:

  • News sites and blogs
  • Wikipedia pages
  • PDFs
  • Online textbooks and articles

It may not work well on pages with mostly images, complex layouts, or dynamic content (like Google Docs).

Use Read Aloud on iPhone or Android

  1. Open the Edge app.
  2. Navigate to the page you want to hear.
  3. Tap the three-dot menu.
  4. Tap Read Aloud.

The app will begin reading, and you’ll see simple playback controls at the bottom of the screen.

This works on most article-style pages and PDFs opened inside Edge.

Final Tip

Combine Read Aloud with Immersive Reader for a clutter-free reading and listening experience. Just click the book icon in the address bar (if available) to enter Immersive Reader, then start Read Aloud from there.

Did you find this article helpful? Let me know by dropping a comment below. If you have questions, ask them away.

item.content:encoded:
Read Aloud in Microsoft Edge can read web pages out loud for you. It’s built in, easy to use, and works great whether you’re multitasking, want to give your eyes a break, or need help with accessibility. Here’s how to use it and make the most of its features.

Start Read Aloud on Any Web Page

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Navigate to a page with text—an article, blog post, or even a PDF.
  3. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  4. Select Read aloud from the menu.

Edge will begin reading the page out loud from the top. A control bar appears at the top of the page.

Use the Read Aloud Controls

The control bar includes:

  • Play/Pause: Start or stop the voice.
  • Skip Back/Forward: Move to the previous or next paragraph.
  • Voice Options: Change the voice and adjust the reading speed.

Click the gear icon to slow down or speed up the voice, or to choose from available voice types like Microsoft Aria or Guy.

Highlight and Start from a Specific Paragraph

If you don’t want to start at the top:

  1. Scroll to the paragraph you want to hear.
  2. Right-click anywhere in the text.
  3. Select Read aloud from here.

Edge will begin reading from that spot.

Use Read Aloud in PDF Files

  1. Open a PDF file in Edge.
  2. Click the Read aloud icon in the toolbar at the top.
  3. Edge will begin reading the text in the PDF, just like it does on web pages.

This is helpful for long documents, reports, or ebooks.

Change the Voice or Speed

  1. While Read Aloud is active, click the Voice options button (a gear icon).
  2. Use the dropdown to pick a different voice.
  3. Adjust the Speed slider to make it faster or slower.

Some voices sound more natural than others, so try a few to find the one you like best.

Keyboard Shortcuts for Read Aloud

  • Ctrl+Shift+U: Start or stop Read Aloud.
  • Use the on-screen buttons for skip, play, or pause.

There’s no shortcut for changing voices—you’ll need to do that manually through the options.

Supported Content

Read Aloud works on:

  • News sites and blogs
  • Wikipedia pages
  • PDFs
  • Online textbooks and articles

It may not work well on pages with mostly images, complex layouts, or dynamic content (like Google Docs).

Use Read Aloud on iPhone or Android

  1. Open the Edge app.
  2. Navigate to the page you want to hear.
  3. Tap the three-dot menu.
  4. Tap Read Aloud.

The app will begin reading, and you’ll see simple playback controls at the bottom of the screen.

This works on most article-style pages and PDFs opened inside Edge.

Final Tip

Combine Read Aloud with Immersive Reader for a clutter-free reading and listening experience. Just click the book icon in the address bar (if available) to enter Immersive Reader, then start Read Aloud from there.

Did you find this article helpful? Let me know by dropping a comment below. If you have questions, ask them away.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: Manual Tab Archiving Coming to Chrome on Android
item.itunes:title: Manual Tab Archiving Coming to Chrome on Android
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Google Chrome on Android is getting a new feature that gives users more control over tab clutter. Right now, Chrome can automatically hide tabs you haven’t used in a while. But soon, you’ll be able to manually archive tabs yourself—no waiting required.

This upcoming feature builds on the existing tab decluttering system. Currently, Chrome hides inactive tabs after 21 days. Those tabs aren’t closed, just tucked away in an “Archived” group that keeps your tab switcher clean. It’s a subtle but useful way to deal with forgotten tabs without losing them.

The new update takes this idea further. A flag spotted in Chromium code—Drag and Drop to Archive Tab—suggests that users will be able to drag a tab in the tab switcher and drop it into the archive area. This lets you manually archive tabs on demand instead of relying on Chrome’s 21-day timer.

Here is the flag I spotted today:

Drag and Drop to Archive Tabs – Enables drag-and-drop tabs in the tab switcher to archive tabs

Manual tab archiving gives users more flexibility. Maybe you’ve finished reading an article but want to keep it for later. Or you’re done comparing prices but want to save those tabs without bookmarking them. Dragging them into the archive gives you a cleaner tab list now, and the option to revisit later.

If you are an Android user, you’ll soon see this feature in action. I am hoping to see this in the Canary version of Chrome in coming weeks.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Google Chrome on Android is getting a new feature that gives users more control over tab clutter. Right now, Chrome can automatically hide tabs you haven’t used in a while. But soon, you’ll be able to manually archive tabs yourself—no waiting required.

This upcoming feature builds on the existing tab decluttering system. Currently, Chrome hides inactive tabs after 21 days. Those tabs aren’t closed, just tucked away in an “Archived” group that keeps your tab switcher clean. It’s a subtle but useful way to deal with forgotten tabs without losing them.

The new update takes this idea further. A flag spotted in Chromium code—Drag and Drop to Archive Tab—suggests that users will be able to drag a tab in the tab switcher and drop it into the archive area. This lets you manually archive tabs on demand instead of relying on Chrome’s 21-day timer.

Here is the flag I spotted today:

Drag and Drop to Archive Tabs – Enables drag-and-drop tabs in the tab switcher to archive tabs

Manual tab archiving gives users more flexibility. Maybe you’ve finished reading an article but want to keep it for later. Or you’re done comparing prices but want to save those tabs without bookmarking them. Dragging them into the archive gives you a cleaner tab list now, and the option to revisit later.

If you are an Android user, you’ll soon see this feature in action. I am hoping to see this in the Canary version of Chrome in coming weeks.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Google Chrome on Android is getting a new feature that gives users more control over tab clutter. Right now, Chrome can automatically hide tabs you haven’t used in a while. But soon, you’ll be able to manually archive tabs yourself—no waiting required.

This upcoming feature builds on the existing tab decluttering system. Currently, Chrome hides inactive tabs after 21 days. Those tabs aren’t closed, just tucked away in an “Archived” group that keeps your tab switcher clean. It’s a subtle but useful way to deal with forgotten tabs without losing them.

The new update takes this idea further. A flag spotted in Chromium code—Drag and Drop to Archive Tab—suggests that users will be able to drag a tab in the tab switcher and drop it into the archive area. This lets you manually archive tabs on demand instead of relying on Chrome’s 21-day timer.

Here is the flag I spotted today:

Drag and Drop to Archive Tabs – Enables drag-and-drop tabs in the tab switcher to archive tabs

Manual tab archiving gives users more flexibility. Maybe you’ve finished reading an article but want to keep it for later. Or you’re done comparing prices but want to save those tabs without bookmarking them. Dragging them into the archive gives you a cleaner tab list now, and the option to revisit later.

If you are an Android user, you’ll soon see this feature in action. I am hoping to see this in the Canary version of Chrome in coming weeks.

Source: Chromium Gerrit.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Clear Cache in Microsoft Edge
item.itunes:title: How to Clear Cache in Microsoft Edge
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Clearing the cache in Microsoft Edge can help fix website issues, speed up your browser, and free up storage space. Here’s how to do it.

Clear Cache Using the Settings Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Select Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Privacy, search, and services.
  5. Scroll down to Clear browsing data and click Choose what to clear.
  6. In the Time range dropdown, pick how far back you want to go—Last hour, Last 24 hours, All time, etc.
  7. Make sure Cached images and files is selected.
  8. Click Clear now.

Edge will remove cached files. This won’t delete your passwords or browsing history unless you select those options too.

Use a Shortcut to Clear Cache Faster

  1. Press Ctrl+Shift+Delete (Windows) or Command+Shift+Delete (Mac).
  2. The Clear browsing data panel opens immediately.
  3. Choose your time range and check Cached images and files.
  4. Click Clear now.

This is a quick way to clear the cache without going through menus.

What Happens When You Clear Cache

  • Edge removes stored versions of websites.
  • Some sites may load more slowly the first time you visit again.
  • You may need to re-enter login information if you also clear cookies.

Bonus Tip: Set Edge to Clear Cache Automatically

  1. Open Settings > Privacy, search, and services.
  2. Scroll to Clear browsing data on close.
  3. Click Choose what to clear every time you close the browser.
  4. Toggle on Cached images and files.

Now Edge will automatically clear the cache when you close it.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Clearing the cache in Microsoft Edge can help fix website issues, speed up your browser, and free up storage space. Here’s how to do it.

Clear Cache Using the Settings Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Select Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Privacy, search, and services.
  5. Scroll down to Clear browsing data and click Choose what to clear.
  6. In the Time range dropdown, pick how far back you want to go—Last hour, Last 24 hours, All time, etc.
  7. Make sure Cached images and files is selected.
  8. Click Clear now.

Edge will remove cached files. This won’t delete your passwords or browsing history unless you select those options too.

Use a Shortcut to Clear Cache Faster

  1. Press Ctrl+Shift+Delete (Windows) or Command+Shift+Delete (Mac).
  2. The Clear browsing data panel opens immediately.
  3. Choose your time range and check Cached images and files.
  4. Click Clear now.

This is a quick way to clear the cache without going through menus.

What Happens When You Clear Cache

  • Edge removes stored versions of websites.
  • Some sites may load more slowly the first time you visit again.
  • You may need to re-enter login information if you also clear cookies.

Bonus Tip: Set Edge to Clear Cache Automatically

  1. Open Settings > Privacy, search, and services.
  2. Scroll to Clear browsing data on close.
  3. Click Choose what to clear every time you close the browser.
  4. Toggle on Cached images and files.

Now Edge will automatically clear the cache when you close it.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Clearing the cache in Microsoft Edge can help fix website issues, speed up your browser, and free up storage space. Here’s how to do it.

Clear Cache Using the Settings Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Select Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Privacy, search, and services.
  5. Scroll down to Clear browsing data and click Choose what to clear.
  6. In the Time range dropdown, pick how far back you want to go—Last hour, Last 24 hours, All time, etc.
  7. Make sure Cached images and files is selected.
  8. Click Clear now.

Edge will remove cached files. This won’t delete your passwords or browsing history unless you select those options too.

Use a Shortcut to Clear Cache Faster

  1. Press Ctrl+Shift+Delete (Windows) or Command+Shift+Delete (Mac).
  2. The Clear browsing data panel opens immediately.
  3. Choose your time range and check Cached images and files.
  4. Click Clear now.

This is a quick way to clear the cache without going through menus.

What Happens When You Clear Cache

  • Edge removes stored versions of websites.
  • Some sites may load more slowly the first time you visit again.
  • You may need to re-enter login information if you also clear cookies.

Bonus Tip: Set Edge to Clear Cache Automatically

  1. Open Settings > Privacy, search, and services.
  2. Scroll to Clear browsing data on close.
  3. Click Choose what to clear every time you close the browser.
  4. Toggle on Cached images and files.

Now Edge will automatically clear the cache when you close it.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Check Your Microsoft Edge Version
item.itunes:title: How to Check Your Microsoft Edge Version
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Knowing your Microsoft Edge version can help you troubleshoot issues, verify compatibility, or make sure you’re running the latest updates. Here’s how to find it in just a few clicks.

Check Edge Version from the Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. From the left sidebar, click About Microsoft Edge.

You’ll see the version number listed at the top of the page. If an update is available, Edge will automatically start downloading it here.

Alternative Method Using Address Bar

  1. Open a new tab in Edge.
  2. Type edge://settings/help in the address bar and press Enter.

This takes you directly to the About page, where the version number is displayed.

Why It Matters

Your version affects which features are available. Some newer features—like split screen or AI integrations—require recent versions. If you’re reporting a bug or following a guide, the version helps ensure you’re seeing the same options.

If you are reaching out to support or a community forum for help, they’ll ask you for the version of Edge for sure.

So, questions? Let me know in the comments section below.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Knowing your Microsoft Edge version can help you troubleshoot issues, verify compatibility, or make sure you’re running the latest updates. Here’s how to find it in just a few clicks.

Check Edge Version from the Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. From the left sidebar, click About Microsoft Edge.

You’ll see the version number listed at the top of the page. If an update is available, Edge will automatically start downloading it here.

Alternative Method Using Address Bar

  1. Open a new tab in Edge.
  2. Type edge://settings/help in the address bar and press Enter.

This takes you directly to the About page, where the version number is displayed.

Why It Matters

Your version affects which features are available. Some newer features—like split screen or AI integrations—require recent versions. If you’re reporting a bug or following a guide, the version helps ensure you’re seeing the same options.

If you are reaching out to support or a community forum for help, they’ll ask you for the version of Edge for sure.

So, questions? Let me know in the comments section below.

item.content:encoded:
Knowing your Microsoft Edge version can help you troubleshoot issues, verify compatibility, or make sure you’re running the latest updates. Here’s how to find it in just a few clicks.

Check Edge Version from the Menu

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) in the top-right corner.
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. From the left sidebar, click About Microsoft Edge.

You’ll see the version number listed at the top of the page. If an update is available, Edge will automatically start downloading it here.

Alternative Method Using Address Bar

  1. Open a new tab in Edge.
  2. Type edge://settings/help in the address bar and press Enter.

This takes you directly to the About page, where the version number is displayed.

Why It Matters

Your version affects which features are available. Some newer features—like split screen or AI integrations—require recent versions. If you’re reporting a bug or following a guide, the version helps ensure you’re seeing the same options.

If you are reaching out to support or a community forum for help, they’ll ask you for the version of Edge for sure.

So, questions? Let me know in the comments section below.

item.title: How to Restore Microsoft Edge to Default Settings
item.itunes:title: How to Restore Microsoft Edge to Default Settings
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you’re having trouble with Microsoft Edge — maybe it’s running slow, behaving oddly, or showing ads and pop-ups you can’t get rid of — restoring the browser to its default settings can help.

This reset doesn’t delete your favorites, history, or saved passwords. It just undoes custom settings, disables extensions, and clears cookies. Here’s how to do it and what to expect.

How to Reset Microsoft Edge to Default

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (top-right corner).
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Reset settings.
  5. Click Restore settings to their default values.
  6. In the confirmation popup, click Reset.

That’s it. Edge will now:

  • Disable all extensions
  • Clear temporary data like cookies
  • Reset your startup page, new tab page, search engine, and pinned tabs

What Is Not Affected

The reset won’t remove:

  • Favorites
  • Browsing history
  • Saved passwords

Those remain intact, so you don’t need to back them up before the reset.

When Should You Reset Edge?

Consider a reset if:

  • Edge is unusually slow
  • You’re getting redirected to unwanted sites
  • Your homepage or search engine has changed without your permission
  • Extensions aren’t behaving as expected
  • You suspect adware or browser hijackers

After the Reset

After resetting, you can:

  • Re-enable the extensions you trust
  • Sign back in to sync settings, history, and passwords
  • Re-customize your startup page and layout preferences

Alternative: Repair or Reinstall Edge

If a reset doesn’t solve your issue, you can repair or reinstall Edge:

On Windows:

  1. Open Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
  2. Search for Microsoft Edge.
  3. Click the three-dot menu next to it.
  4. Choose Modify > then click Repair.

This will reinstall Edge without affecting your data.

Summary

Resetting Microsoft Edge is a simple, safe way to fix most browser issues. It keeps your important data like favorites and passwords while removing problematic settings or extensions. If you still see issues after the reset, try a repair or reinstall for a clean slate.

Questions? Drop them in the comments form below.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
If you’re having trouble with Microsoft Edge — maybe it’s running slow, behaving oddly, or showing ads and pop-ups you can’t get rid of — restoring the browser to its default settings can help.

This reset doesn’t delete your favorites, history, or saved passwords. It just undoes custom settings, disables extensions, and clears cookies. Here’s how to do it and what to expect.

How to Reset Microsoft Edge to Default

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (top-right corner).
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Reset settings.
  5. Click Restore settings to their default values.
  6. In the confirmation popup, click Reset.

That’s it. Edge will now:

  • Disable all extensions
  • Clear temporary data like cookies
  • Reset your startup page, new tab page, search engine, and pinned tabs

What Is Not Affected

The reset won’t remove:

  • Favorites
  • Browsing history
  • Saved passwords

Those remain intact, so you don’t need to back them up before the reset.

When Should You Reset Edge?

Consider a reset if:

  • Edge is unusually slow
  • You’re getting redirected to unwanted sites
  • Your homepage or search engine has changed without your permission
  • Extensions aren’t behaving as expected
  • You suspect adware or browser hijackers

After the Reset

After resetting, you can:

  • Re-enable the extensions you trust
  • Sign back in to sync settings, history, and passwords
  • Re-customize your startup page and layout preferences

Alternative: Repair or Reinstall Edge

If a reset doesn’t solve your issue, you can repair or reinstall Edge:

On Windows:

  1. Open Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
  2. Search for Microsoft Edge.
  3. Click the three-dot menu next to it.
  4. Choose Modify > then click Repair.

This will reinstall Edge without affecting your data.

Summary

Resetting Microsoft Edge is a simple, safe way to fix most browser issues. It keeps your important data like favorites and passwords while removing problematic settings or extensions. If you still see issues after the reset, try a repair or reinstall for a clean slate.

Questions? Drop them in the comments form below.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
If you’re having trouble with Microsoft Edge — maybe it’s running slow, behaving oddly, or showing ads and pop-ups you can’t get rid of — restoring the browser to its default settings can help.

This reset doesn’t delete your favorites, history, or saved passwords. It just undoes custom settings, disables extensions, and clears cookies. Here’s how to do it and what to expect.

How to Reset Microsoft Edge to Default

  1. Open Microsoft Edge.
  2. Click the three-dot menu (top-right corner).
  3. Go to Settings.
  4. In the left sidebar, click Reset settings.
  5. Click Restore settings to their default values.
  6. In the confirmation popup, click Reset.

That’s it. Edge will now:

  • Disable all extensions
  • Clear temporary data like cookies
  • Reset your startup page, new tab page, search engine, and pinned tabs

What Is Not Affected

The reset won’t remove:

  • Favorites
  • Browsing history
  • Saved passwords

Those remain intact, so you don’t need to back them up before the reset.

When Should You Reset Edge?

Consider a reset if:

  • Edge is unusually slow
  • You’re getting redirected to unwanted sites
  • Your homepage or search engine has changed without your permission
  • Extensions aren’t behaving as expected
  • You suspect adware or browser hijackers

After the Reset

After resetting, you can:

  • Re-enable the extensions you trust
  • Sign back in to sync settings, history, and passwords
  • Re-customize your startup page and layout preferences

Alternative: Repair or Reinstall Edge

If a reset doesn’t solve your issue, you can repair or reinstall Edge:

On Windows:

  1. Open Settings > Apps > Installed apps.
  2. Search for Microsoft Edge.
  3. Click the three-dot menu next to it.
  4. Choose Modify > then click Repair.

This will reinstall Edge without affecting your data.

Summary

Resetting Microsoft Edge is a simple, safe way to fix most browser issues. It keeps your important data like favorites and passwords while removing problematic settings or extensions. If you still see issues after the reset, try a repair or reinstall for a clean slate.

Questions? Drop them in the comments form below.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Use Favorites in Microsoft Edge: The Complete Guide
item.itunes:title: How to Use Favorites in Microsoft Edge: The Complete Guide
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Favorites in Microsoft Edge let you quickly access your go-to websites. Whether you’re researching, reading, shopping, or working, saving pages as favorites means you don’t have to search for them again later. In this guide, we’ll cover everything you need to know about using Favorites in Edge—on both Edge for computers, iPhones, and Android devices.

What Are Favorites in Microsoft Edge?

Favorites are Edge’s version of bookmarks. They let you save any webpage and revisit it later with a click. Your favorites sync across devices if you’re signed in with a Microsoft account, making it easy to pick up where you left off.

How to Add a Page to Favorites in Edge

  1. Open Microsoft Edge and navigate to the page you want to save.
  2. Click the star icon in the address bar. It’s on the right side.
  3. A small panel appears—choose a name for the favorite or leave the default.
  4. Pick a folder if you’ve set any up, or leave it in the default folder.
  5. Click Done.

You’ve just added a favorite. You can repeat this for any page you want to revisit later.

How to Access Your Favorites in Edge

  1. Click the star icon with three horizontal lines (called the Favorites menu) on the toolbar.
  2. A side panel opens showing all your saved favorites.
  3. Click any link to open the site in a new tab.

Alternatively, you can use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+O to open the Favorites panel quickly.

How to Edit a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Find the favorite you want to edit.
  3. Right-click it and select Edit.
  4. You can change the name, URL, or folder.
  5. Click Save.

You can also drag and drop the favorite to a new folder right in the Favorites panel.

How to Delete a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Right-click the favorite you want to remove.
  3. Select Delete.

Deleted favorites are not recoverable unless you backed them up or synced them with a Microsoft account.

How to Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Right-click in an empty space and select Create new folder.
  3. Name the folder.
  4. Drag favorites into the folder to group them.

You can also create folders when adding a favorite. In the panel that appears, click the drop-down and select Add new folder.

How to Search Favorites in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Use the search bar at the top to find saved items quickly.

This is helpful if you’ve saved a lot of pages and can’t remember where you filed something.

How to Sort and Rearrange Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Drag and drop entries to reorder them.
  3. Right-click a folder or favorite to rename or move it.

There’s no auto-sort, but manual organization works well for most users.

How to Sync Favorites Across Devices

  1. Click the three-dot menu in Edge.
  2. Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync.
  3. Make sure Favorites is toggled on.

Now your favorites will be available on any device where you’re signed in with the same Microsoft account.

How to Use the Favorites Bar

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Appearance.
  2. Toggle Show favorites bar to either:
    • Always
    • Only on new tabs
    • Never

Drag your most-used favorites to this bar for even faster access.

How to Import Favorites from Another Browser

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Profiles > Import browser data.
  2. Choose a browser and check Favorites or bookmarks.
  3. Click Import.

This pulls in all saved favorites from other browsers like Chrome or Firefox.

How to Export Favorites

Edge doesn’t offer a direct export from the menu, but here’s a workaround:

  1. Go to edge://favorites in the address bar.
  2. Click the three-dot menu in the panel.
  3. Choose Export favorites.

This saves a .html file you can import elsewhere.

How to Restore Deleted Favorites

If you’ve recently deleted a favorite and sync is enabled, there’s a chance it’s recoverable:

  • Sign into your Microsoft account at https://account.microsoft.com
  • Check if your favorites are listed under Edge sync
  • If synced, try signing out and back in to reload from the cloud

How to Use Favorites in Edge on iPhone

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and go to a site.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom.
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Choose a name and folder, then tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the menu icon.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse or search your saved sites.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Go to the Favorites list.
  2. Tap Edit.
  3. Select an item to rename or delete.

Organize into Folders

  1. In the Favorites list, tap New Folder.
  2. Move items into folders by editing their location.

Favorites on iPhone also sync with Edge on your computer if you’re signed in.

How to Use Favorites in Edge on Android

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and visit a page you want to save.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom or top (depending on your layout).
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Rename it if needed, pick a folder, and tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the three-dot menu.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse through the folders or use the search bar at the top.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Tap and hold the favorite you want to change.
  3. Choose Edit to rename or move it, or Delete to remove it.

Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Inside the Favorites section, tap New Folder.
  2. Drag or edit existing favorites to place them in folders.

Your Android favorites also sync with other devices when you’re signed into the same Microsoft account.

Advanced Tips for Using Favorites

Use keyword nicknames: Rename your favorites with short, memorable keywords so you can easily search and open them.

Pin folders to the Start menu (Windows only): You can right-click a folder in your Favorites and choose to open it as a new tab group or pin specific favorites as app-like tiles.

Use Edge Collections for more advanced needs: If you’re organizing notes or want to group webpages with comments and annotations, try using Collections in Edge. It works alongside Favorites but offers more flexibility.

Backup Favorites manually: Even if you sync, it’s smart to occasionally export your favorites as an HTML file from edge://favorites, just in case.

Use extensions for better management: Search the Edge Add-ons store for bookmark managers that can offer additional sorting, tagging, or backup features.

Organize by task or goal: Instead of categories like News or Social, consider grouping by actions—like Daily Read, Research Project, or Shopping List.

Declutter regularly: Set a reminder every month or quarter to delete outdated favorites, rename vague ones, and make sure your folders are still relevant.

Troubleshooting Favorites in Edge

Favorites not syncing:

  • Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync and ensure Favorites is turned on.
  • Try turning sync off and back on.
  • Sign out and sign in again to refresh your sync status.

Missing Favorites after update:

  • Visit edge://favorites to confirm if they’re truly gone.
  • If you exported a backup before, import it again.
  • Check other synced devices or Microsoft’s sync portal.

Can’t add a new favorite:

  • Make sure you’re signed in.
  • Restart Edge to refresh the browser state.
  • Clear cache and cookies if problems persist.

Import/export options not working:

  • Close all other browsers while importing.
  • Try exporting from the edge://favorites page instead of the menu.

Summary

Microsoft Edge’s Favorites feature is more powerful than it first appears. With the right setup, you can save time, reduce clutter, and keep everything you need just a tap or click away. Whether you’re using Edge on your computer, iPhone, or Android device, Favorites help make browsing more efficient and personalized.

Questions? Ask them in the comments form below and I’ll do my best to get you an answer.

Do you have additional favorites tips? Drop them as well here.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Favorites in Microsoft Edge let you quickly access your go-to websites. Whether you’re researching, reading, shopping, or working, saving pages as favorites means you don’t have to search for them again later. In this guide, we’ll cover everything you need to know about using Favorites in Edge—on both Edge for computers, iPhones, and Android devices.

What Are Favorites in Microsoft Edge?

Favorites are Edge’s version of bookmarks. They let you save any webpage and revisit it later with a click. Your favorites sync across devices if you’re signed in with a Microsoft account, making it easy to pick up where you left off.

How to Add a Page to Favorites in Edge

  1. Open Microsoft Edge and navigate to the page you want to save.
  2. Click the star icon in the address bar. It’s on the right side.
  3. A small panel appears—choose a name for the favorite or leave the default.
  4. Pick a folder if you’ve set any up, or leave it in the default folder.
  5. Click Done.

You’ve just added a favorite. You can repeat this for any page you want to revisit later.

How to Access Your Favorites in Edge

  1. Click the star icon with three horizontal lines (called the Favorites menu) on the toolbar.
  2. A side panel opens showing all your saved favorites.
  3. Click any link to open the site in a new tab.

Alternatively, you can use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+O to open the Favorites panel quickly.

How to Edit a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Find the favorite you want to edit.
  3. Right-click it and select Edit.
  4. You can change the name, URL, or folder.
  5. Click Save.

You can also drag and drop the favorite to a new folder right in the Favorites panel.

How to Delete a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Right-click the favorite you want to remove.
  3. Select Delete.

Deleted favorites are not recoverable unless you backed them up or synced them with a Microsoft account.

How to Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Right-click in an empty space and select Create new folder.
  3. Name the folder.
  4. Drag favorites into the folder to group them.

You can also create folders when adding a favorite. In the panel that appears, click the drop-down and select Add new folder.

How to Search Favorites in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Use the search bar at the top to find saved items quickly.

This is helpful if you’ve saved a lot of pages and can’t remember where you filed something.

How to Sort and Rearrange Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Drag and drop entries to reorder them.
  3. Right-click a folder or favorite to rename or move it.

There’s no auto-sort, but manual organization works well for most users.

How to Sync Favorites Across Devices

  1. Click the three-dot menu in Edge.
  2. Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync.
  3. Make sure Favorites is toggled on.

Now your favorites will be available on any device where you’re signed in with the same Microsoft account.

How to Use the Favorites Bar

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Appearance.
  2. Toggle Show favorites bar to either:
    • Always
    • Only on new tabs
    • Never

Drag your most-used favorites to this bar for even faster access.

How to Import Favorites from Another Browser

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Profiles > Import browser data.
  2. Choose a browser and check Favorites or bookmarks.
  3. Click Import.

This pulls in all saved favorites from other browsers like Chrome or Firefox.

How to Export Favorites

Edge doesn’t offer a direct export from the menu, but here’s a workaround:

  1. Go to edge://favorites in the address bar.
  2. Click the three-dot menu in the panel.
  3. Choose Export favorites.

This saves a .html file you can import elsewhere.

How to Restore Deleted Favorites

If you’ve recently deleted a favorite and sync is enabled, there’s a chance it’s recoverable:

  • Sign into your Microsoft account at https://account.microsoft.com
  • Check if your favorites are listed under Edge sync
  • If synced, try signing out and back in to reload from the cloud

How to Use Favorites in Edge on iPhone

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and go to a site.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom.
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Choose a name and folder, then tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the menu icon.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse or search your saved sites.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Go to the Favorites list.
  2. Tap Edit.
  3. Select an item to rename or delete.

Organize into Folders

  1. In the Favorites list, tap New Folder.
  2. Move items into folders by editing their location.

Favorites on iPhone also sync with Edge on your computer if you’re signed in.

How to Use Favorites in Edge on Android

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and visit a page you want to save.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom or top (depending on your layout).
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Rename it if needed, pick a folder, and tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the three-dot menu.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse through the folders or use the search bar at the top.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Tap and hold the favorite you want to change.
  3. Choose Edit to rename or move it, or Delete to remove it.

Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Inside the Favorites section, tap New Folder.
  2. Drag or edit existing favorites to place them in folders.

Your Android favorites also sync with other devices when you’re signed into the same Microsoft account.

Advanced Tips for Using Favorites

Use keyword nicknames: Rename your favorites with short, memorable keywords so you can easily search and open them.

Pin folders to the Start menu (Windows only): You can right-click a folder in your Favorites and choose to open it as a new tab group or pin specific favorites as app-like tiles.

Use Edge Collections for more advanced needs: If you’re organizing notes or want to group webpages with comments and annotations, try using Collections in Edge. It works alongside Favorites but offers more flexibility.

Backup Favorites manually: Even if you sync, it’s smart to occasionally export your favorites as an HTML file from edge://favorites, just in case.

Use extensions for better management: Search the Edge Add-ons store for bookmark managers that can offer additional sorting, tagging, or backup features.

Organize by task or goal: Instead of categories like News or Social, consider grouping by actions—like Daily Read, Research Project, or Shopping List.

Declutter regularly: Set a reminder every month or quarter to delete outdated favorites, rename vague ones, and make sure your folders are still relevant.

Troubleshooting Favorites in Edge

Favorites not syncing:

  • Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync and ensure Favorites is turned on.
  • Try turning sync off and back on.
  • Sign out and sign in again to refresh your sync status.

Missing Favorites after update:

  • Visit edge://favorites to confirm if they’re truly gone.
  • If you exported a backup before, import it again.
  • Check other synced devices or Microsoft’s sync portal.

Can’t add a new favorite:

  • Make sure you’re signed in.
  • Restart Edge to refresh the browser state.
  • Clear cache and cookies if problems persist.

Import/export options not working:

  • Close all other browsers while importing.
  • Try exporting from the edge://favorites page instead of the menu.

Summary

Microsoft Edge’s Favorites feature is more powerful than it first appears. With the right setup, you can save time, reduce clutter, and keep everything you need just a tap or click away. Whether you’re using Edge on your computer, iPhone, or Android device, Favorites help make browsing more efficient and personalized.

Questions? Ask them in the comments form below and I’ll do my best to get you an answer.

Do you have additional favorites tips? Drop them as well here.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Favorites in Microsoft Edge let you quickly access your go-to websites. Whether you’re researching, reading, shopping, or working, saving pages as favorites means you don’t have to search for them again later. In this guide, we’ll cover everything you need to know about using Favorites in Edge—on both Edge for computers, iPhones, and Android devices.

What Are Favorites in Microsoft Edge?

Favorites are Edge’s version of bookmarks. They let you save any webpage and revisit it later with a click. Your favorites sync across devices if you’re signed in with a Microsoft account, making it easy to pick up where you left off.

How to Add a Page to Favorites in Edge

  1. Open Microsoft Edge and navigate to the page you want to save.
  2. Click the star icon in the address bar. It’s on the right side.
  3. A small panel appears—choose a name for the favorite or leave the default.
  4. Pick a folder if you’ve set any up, or leave it in the default folder.
  5. Click Done.

You’ve just added a favorite. You can repeat this for any page you want to revisit later.

How to Access Your Favorites in Edge

  1. Click the star icon with three horizontal lines (called the Favorites menu) on the toolbar.
  2. A side panel opens showing all your saved favorites.
  3. Click any link to open the site in a new tab.

Alternatively, you can use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+O to open the Favorites panel quickly.

How to Edit a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Find the favorite you want to edit.
  3. Right-click it and select Edit.
  4. You can change the name, URL, or folder.
  5. Click Save.

You can also drag and drop the favorite to a new folder right in the Favorites panel.

How to Delete a Favorite in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Right-click the favorite you want to remove.
  3. Select Delete.

Deleted favorites are not recoverable unless you backed them up or synced them with a Microsoft account.

How to Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Right-click in an empty space and select Create new folder.
  3. Name the folder.
  4. Drag favorites into the folder to group them.

You can also create folders when adding a favorite. In the panel that appears, click the drop-down and select Add new folder.

How to Search Favorites in Edge

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Use the search bar at the top to find saved items quickly.

This is helpful if you’ve saved a lot of pages and can’t remember where you filed something.

How to Sort and Rearrange Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites panel.
  2. Drag and drop entries to reorder them.
  3. Right-click a folder or favorite to rename or move it.

There’s no auto-sort, but manual organization works well for most users.

How to Sync Favorites Across Devices

  1. Click the three-dot menu in Edge.
  2. Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync.
  3. Make sure Favorites is toggled on.

Now your favorites will be available on any device where you’re signed in with the same Microsoft account.

How to Use the Favorites Bar

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Appearance.
  2. Toggle Show favorites bar to either:
    • Always
    • Only on new tabs
    • Never

Drag your most-used favorites to this bar for even faster access.

How to Import Favorites from Another Browser

  1. Click the three-dot menu > Settings > Profiles > Import browser data.
  2. Choose a browser and check Favorites or bookmarks.
  3. Click Import.

This pulls in all saved favorites from other browsers like Chrome or Firefox.

How to Export Favorites

Edge doesn’t offer a direct export from the menu, but here’s a workaround:

  1. Go to edge://favorites in the address bar.
  2. Click the three-dot menu in the panel.
  3. Choose Export favorites.

This saves a .html file you can import elsewhere.

How to Restore Deleted Favorites

If you’ve recently deleted a favorite and sync is enabled, there’s a chance it’s recoverable:

  • Sign into your Microsoft account at https://account.microsoft.com
  • Check if your favorites are listed under Edge sync
  • If synced, try signing out and back in to reload from the cloud

How to Use Favorites in Edge on iPhone

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and go to a site.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom.
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Choose a name and folder, then tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the menu icon.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse or search your saved sites.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Go to the Favorites list.
  2. Tap Edit.
  3. Select an item to rename or delete.

Organize into Folders

  1. In the Favorites list, tap New Folder.
  2. Move items into folders by editing their location.

Favorites on iPhone also sync with Edge on your computer if you’re signed in.

How to Use Favorites in Edge on Android

Add a Page to Favorites

  1. Open the Edge app and visit a page you want to save.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu at the bottom or top (depending on your layout).
  3. Tap the star icon.
  4. Rename it if needed, pick a folder, and tap Save.

Access Favorites

  1. Tap the three-dot menu.
  2. Tap Favorites.
  3. Browse through the folders or use the search bar at the top.

Edit or Delete Favorites

  1. Open the Favorites menu.
  2. Tap and hold the favorite you want to change.
  3. Choose Edit to rename or move it, or Delete to remove it.

Organize Favorites into Folders

  1. Inside the Favorites section, tap New Folder.
  2. Drag or edit existing favorites to place them in folders.

Your Android favorites also sync with other devices when you’re signed into the same Microsoft account.

Advanced Tips for Using Favorites

Use keyword nicknames: Rename your favorites with short, memorable keywords so you can easily search and open them.

Pin folders to the Start menu (Windows only): You can right-click a folder in your Favorites and choose to open it as a new tab group or pin specific favorites as app-like tiles.

Use Edge Collections for more advanced needs: If you’re organizing notes or want to group webpages with comments and annotations, try using Collections in Edge. It works alongside Favorites but offers more flexibility.

Backup Favorites manually: Even if you sync, it’s smart to occasionally export your favorites as an HTML file from edge://favorites, just in case.

Use extensions for better management: Search the Edge Add-ons store for bookmark managers that can offer additional sorting, tagging, or backup features.

Organize by task or goal: Instead of categories like News or Social, consider grouping by actions—like Daily Read, Research Project, or Shopping List.

Declutter regularly: Set a reminder every month or quarter to delete outdated favorites, rename vague ones, and make sure your folders are still relevant.

Troubleshooting Favorites in Edge

Favorites not syncing:

  • Go to Settings > Profiles > Sync and ensure Favorites is turned on.
  • Try turning sync off and back on.
  • Sign out and sign in again to refresh your sync status.

Missing Favorites after update:

  • Visit edge://favorites to confirm if they’re truly gone.
  • If you exported a backup before, import it again.
  • Check other synced devices or Microsoft’s sync portal.

Can’t add a new favorite:

  • Make sure you’re signed in.
  • Restart Edge to refresh the browser state.
  • Clear cache and cookies if problems persist.

Import/export options not working:

  • Close all other browsers while importing.
  • Try exporting from the edge://favorites page instead of the menu.

Summary

Microsoft Edge’s Favorites feature is more powerful than it first appears. With the right setup, you can save time, reduce clutter, and keep everything you need just a tap or click away. Whether you’re using Edge on your computer, iPhone, or Android device, Favorites help make browsing more efficient and personalized.

Questions? Ask them in the comments form below and I’ll do my best to get you an answer.

Do you have additional favorites tips? Drop them as well here.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Access Your Bookmarks in Chrome on iPhone
item.itunes:title: How to Access Your Bookmarks in Chrome on iPhone
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Chrome on iPhone doesn’t make bookmarks as visible as you’d expect. There’s no bookmarks bar like on desktop, and the option is tucked away in a menu. If you’re trying to get back to something you saved earlier, here’s exactly where to find it.

  1. Open the Chrome app
    Tap the Chrome icon on your iPhone to launch it.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu
    Look at the bottom-right corner and tap the three dots (⋯) to open the menu.
  3. Tap “Bookmarks”
    From the list, choose Bookmarks. This opens your saved links.
  4. Choose a folder
    You’ll see folders like “Mobile Bookmarks” or “Bookmarks Bar.” Tap a folder to view its contents.

Once you tap a bookmark, Chrome loads that page. If your list looks empty, make sure you’re signed into the right Google account and sync is enabled in settings.

Questions? Let me know in the comments section.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Chrome on iPhone doesn’t make bookmarks as visible as you’d expect. There’s no bookmarks bar like on desktop, and the option is tucked away in a menu. If you’re trying to get back to something you saved earlier, here’s exactly where to find it.

  1. Open the Chrome app
    Tap the Chrome icon on your iPhone to launch it.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu
    Look at the bottom-right corner and tap the three dots (⋯) to open the menu.
  3. Tap “Bookmarks”
    From the list, choose Bookmarks. This opens your saved links.
  4. Choose a folder
    You’ll see folders like “Mobile Bookmarks” or “Bookmarks Bar.” Tap a folder to view its contents.

Once you tap a bookmark, Chrome loads that page. If your list looks empty, make sure you’re signed into the right Google account and sync is enabled in settings.

Questions? Let me know in the comments section.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.content:encoded:
Chrome on iPhone doesn’t make bookmarks as visible as you’d expect. There’s no bookmarks bar like on desktop, and the option is tucked away in a menu. If you’re trying to get back to something you saved earlier, here’s exactly where to find it.

  1. Open the Chrome app
    Tap the Chrome icon on your iPhone to launch it.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu
    Look at the bottom-right corner and tap the three dots (⋯) to open the menu.
  3. Tap “Bookmarks”
    From the list, choose Bookmarks. This opens your saved links.
  4. Choose a folder
    You’ll see folders like “Mobile Bookmarks” or “Bookmarks Bar.” Tap a folder to view its contents.

Once you tap a bookmark, Chrome loads that page. If your list looks empty, make sure you’re signed into the right Google account and sync is enabled in settings.

Questions? Let me know in the comments section.

Like this:

Like Loading…

item.title: How to Sign Out of Google Chrome (Windows, macOS, Linux)
item.itunes:title: How to Sign Out of Google Chrome (Windows, macOS, Linux)
item.itunes:author: Chrome Story[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Looking for a way to log out of Google Chrome? Here is how to do that if you are on desktop, or mobile.

From Chrome Settings

  1. Open Chrome.
  2. Go to the address bar and type: chrome://settings
  3. Under the “You and Google” section, click “Turn off” next to your name and email.
  4. Confirm sign-out and data handling.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on Android

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu > Settings.
  3. Tap your Google account at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out and turn off sync”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on iPhone or iPad

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu (bottom right) > Settings.
  3. Tap your name at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out of Chrome”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

What Happens When You Sign Out

  • Sync stops across your devices.
  • Your bookmarks, passwords, history, and other data stay on the device (unless you choose to remove them).
  • You’ll stay signed into other Google services in the browser unless you sign out separately at google.com.

Questions ? Let me know in the comments form below.

Like this:

Like Loading…
item.description:
Looking for a way to log out of Google Chrome? Here is how to do that if you are on desktop, or mobile.

From Chrome Settings

  1. Open Chrome.
  2. Go to the address bar and type: chrome://settings
  3. Under the “You and Google” section, click “Turn off” next to your name and email.
  4. Confirm sign-out and data handling.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on Android

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu > Settings.
  3. Tap your Google account at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out and turn off sync”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on iPhone or iPad

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu (bottom right) > Settings.
  3. Tap your name at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out of Chrome”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

What Happens When You Sign Out

  • Sync stops across your devices.
  • Your bookmarks, passwords, history, and other data stay on the device (unless you choose to remove them).
  • You’ll stay signed into other Google services in the browser unless you sign out separately at google.com.

Questions ? Let me know in the comments form below.

item.content:encoded:
Looking for a way to log out of Google Chrome? Here is how to do that if you are on desktop, or mobile.

From Chrome Settings

  1. Open Chrome.
  2. Go to the address bar and type: chrome://settings
  3. Under the “You and Google” section, click “Turn off” next to your name and email.
  4. Confirm sign-out and data handling.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on Android

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu > Settings.
  3. Tap your Google account at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out and turn off sync”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

How to Sign Out of Google Chrome on iPhone or iPad

  1. Open the Chrome app.
  2. Tap the three-dot menu (bottom right) > Settings.
  3. Tap your name at the top.
  4. Tap “Sign out of Chrome”.
  5. Confirm the sign-out.

What Happens When You Sign Out

  • Sync stops across your devices.
  • Your bookmarks, passwords, history, and other data stay on the device (unless you choose to remove them).
  • You’ll stay signed into other Google services in the browser unless you sign out separately at google.com.

Questions ? Let me know in the comments form below.
